subject: UX & Research
title: Cross-Cultural Testing Challenges
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Cross-Cultural Testing Challenges.

hook.text: A thumbs-up emoji means 'great' in the US. In parts of the Middle East, it's offensive. Test globally.

buildup.text: Colors, gestures, and layouts carry cultural meaning that designers from one culture can easily miss.

discovery.text: In Japan, users rarely express negative opinions in testing. Silence means politeness, not satisfaction.

twist.text: Translation isn't localization. A Spanish version might use correct grammar that nobody in Mexico would say.

climax.text: Test with local users in their language, on their devices, in their context. Remote tools make this feasible.

punchline.text: Same product, different cultures, different problems. Test locally.

quiz.question: Why is silence during testing with Japanese users not a reliable signal?
options: Japanese culture values politeness — silence often means courtesy, not satisfaction
Japanese users always give verbal feedback
Silence indicates the test recording equipment failed
